question: A connector adds the `connector-validation` artifact and annotates its request class with `@NotEmpty` on `message` and `@NotNull @Valid` on a nested `authentication`. When does the SDK actually run the validator on the input data?

options.text: Only when the developer explicitly calls `Validator.validate(input)` before reading variables.
Automatically when the connector calls `bindVariables` (outbound) or `bindProperties` (inbound) on its connector context.
Only at modeler deploy time; runtime input data is never re-validated.
Only after the connector function returns a result; the validator inspects the result for missing fields.

a.text: The docs say validation is performed automatically through `bindVariables` / `bindProperties`; an explicit `Validator.validate` call is not required.

b.text: The Validation section of the SDK docs states: "Validation is performed automatically if you use the `bindVariables` / `bindProperties` methods." The context wires up a Hibernate-Validator-backed Jakarta Bean Validation provider and applies the annotations, including `@Valid` to recurse into nested object graphs like `authentication`.

c.text: Element templates can carry some `constraints` for modeling-time validation, but the runtime SDK validation (via `connector-validation`) runs against the input data passed to the function, not at deploy time.

d.text: The validator inspects the request object that you bind from the context, not the result your function returns.

explanation: Adding the `connector-validation` artifact wires a Jakarta Bean Validation provider (Hibernate Validator) into the SDK. When your connector calls `context.bindVariables(...)` (outbound) or `context.bindProperties(...)` (inbound), the SDK deserializes the input into your Java class, replaces secrets, and runs the validator across your annotations automatically. `@Valid` causes the validator to recurse into nested objects like `authentication`.
